ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To John, Bishop.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to John, Bishop of Ravenna.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: In the first place this makes me sad; that thy Fraternity writes to me with a double heart, exhibiting one sort of blandishment in letters, but another sort with the tongue in secular intercourse.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: In the next place, it grieves me that my brother John even to this day retains on his tongue those gibes which notaries while still boys are wont to indulge in.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: He speaks bitingly, and seems to delight in such pleasantry.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: He flatters his friends in their presence, and maligns them in their absence.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Thirdly, it is to me grievous and altogether execrable, that he imputes shameful crimes to his servants, whatever the hour may be, calling them “effeminate;” and, what is still more grievous, this is done openly.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Then there is this in addition that there is no discipline for keeping guard over the life of the clergy, but that he exhibits himself only as their lord.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: The last thing, but first in importance as evidence of elation, is about his use of the pallium outside the church, which is a thing he never presumed to do in the times of my predecessors, and what none of his predecessors ever presumed to do, as our delegates testify (except it might be when relics were deposited, though with regard to relics one person only could be found to say that it was so); yet this in my days, in contempt of me, with extreme audacity, he not only did, but even made a habit of doing.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: From all these things I find that the dignity of the Episcopacy is with him all in outside show, not in his mind.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And indeed I return thanks to Almighty God that at the time when this came to my knowledge, which had never reached the ears of my predecessors, the Lombards were posted between me and the city of Ravenna.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For perchance I had it in my mind to shew to men how severe I can be.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Lest, however, thou shouldest suppose that I wish thy church to be depressed or lessened in dignity, remember where the deacon of Ravenna used to stand in solemnization of mass at Rome, and enquire where he stands now; and thou wilt recognize the fact that I desire to honour the church of Ravenna.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But that any one whatever should snatch at anything out of pride, this I cannot tolerate.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Nevertheless I have already written on this matter to our deacon at Constantinople, that he should enquire of all who have under them even thirty or forty bishops.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And if there is anywhere this custom of their walking in litanies wearing the pallium, God forbid that through me the dignity of the church of Ravenna should seem to be in any way lessened.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Reflect, therefore, dearest brother, on all that I have said above: think of the day of thy call: consider what account thou wilt render of the burden of episcopacy.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Amend those manners of a notary.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: See what becomes a bishop in tongue and in deed.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Be entirely sincere to thy brethren.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Do not speak one thing, and have another in thy heart.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Do not desire to seem more than thou art, that so thou mayest be able to be more than thou seemest.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Believe me, when I came to my present position, I had such consideration and charity towards thee that, if thou hadst wished to keep hold of this my charity, thou still wouldest not have ever found such a brother as myself, or one so sincerely loving thee, or so concurring with thee in all devotion: but when I came to know of thy words and thy manners, I confess I started back.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I beseech thee, then, by Almighty God, amend all that I have spoken of, and especially the vice of duplicity.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Allow me to love thee; and for the present and the future life it may be of advantage to thee to be loved of thy brethren.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Reply, however, to all this, not by words, but by behaviour.
